reasoning:
Federal Government Current Expenditures (FGEXPND) normally grows year-over-year due to mandatory spending (Social Security, Medicare) and increasing interest payments on the national debt. Nominal spending rarely declines outside of major post-war or post-stimulus environments. For the resolution to be YES, nominal spending in Q4 2025 would need to be at least $250 billion LOWER than Q4 2024. Therefore, any cuts from Elon Musk and the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) would need to completely offset the natural baseline growth (which typically ranges from $200B-$400B annually) AND cut an additional $250B on top of that. 

News reports up to October 2025 repeatedly indicate that DOGE's ambitions have severely scaled back. Initially promising up to $2 trillion in cuts, Musk and DOGE adjusted their realistic savings down to around $150 billion to $190 billion, primarily targeting smaller agencies that have minimal macro-level effect on current expenditures. Considering that the target savings figures themselves ($150-$190B) are less than the required $250 billion drop—and do not even account for offsetting the natural baseline growth—it is almost mathematically impossible for the final Q4 2025 FRED data print to fall by more than $250 billion nominally compared to Q4 2024.
